instruction:
Given that $\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-x^{2}} \, d x \,=\, {\frac{1} {2}} \sqrt{\pi}$. Using complex calculus to calculate inappropriate integrals $\int_{0}^{\infty} \operatorname{s i n} ( x^{2} ) \, d x$ and $\int_{0}^{\infty} \operatorname{c o s} ( x^{2} ) \, d x$ both converge to ().

Options:
A. $$
\frac{\pi}{4} e.
$$
B. $$
\pi \sqrt{2/3}.
$$
C. $$
\frac{4\pi}{3 \sqrt{2}}.
$$
D. $$
\sqrt{\pi/ 8}.
$$
E. $$
\frac{\pi}{3 \sqrt{2}}.
$$
F. $$
\sqrt{\pi} e^{2}.
$$
G. $$
e^{\pi/2}.
$$
H. $$
\sqrt{2\pi} e.
$$
I. $$
\frac{e \pi^{2}}{8}.
$$
J. $$
\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{6}.
$$
